text.title: Scope-Creep Email Language Pack Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every push-back email must follow so the response is firm without being adversarial and converts creep into change orders instead of absorbed work.

rule.statement: The first sentence acknowledges the request as legitimate and useful before any push-back. Replies that open with "this is out of scope" or "we did not agree to" are rejected; rephrase to lead with "this is a useful idea — here is how we can do it cleanly".
rule.rationale: Opening with a refusal triggers defensiveness regardless of how the rest of the message reads. Acknowledging first preserves the relationship and lets the substance land; this is purely a phrasing rule, not a substance rule — the answer can still be "no".

rule.statement: Every push-back must explicitly name "this is a change to the agreed scope" and offer a concrete next step (change-order link, rate, calendar slot to scope it). "Sorry, we can't" alone is rejected. The change-order framing turns "no" into "yes, here is how" without absorbing.
rule.rationale: Bare refusals teach clients to escalate or work around the freelancer. Reframing converts the same conversation into a commercial one with a clear path, which is the only outcome that preserves both relationship and margin.

rule.statement: The language pack must NOT contain a default "we'll just include this one" phrasing. The closest allowed phrasing is "we'll log this as goodwill for engagement <id> and review at next checkpoint" — the goodwill must be named, logged, and budget-capped (max 5% of engagement value across the engagement).
rule.rationale: Absorbing once teaches the client that asking works. The named-goodwill phrasing preserves the option (sometimes worth it) but caps the cumulative absorption and surfaces the pattern at the next checkpoint so the founder can re-price.

rule.statement: Per-client send-log must track which phrasing has been used with this client. The same exact phrasing must not be sent to the same client twice in a row; if no fresh option exists, the founder edits the phrasing or escalates to a call.
rule.rationale: Identical phrasing twice tells the client they are talking to a template, not a partner. Rotating preserves the polite-firm effect while keeping the relationship human. The "escalate to a call" fallback covers the case where the founder is out of phrasings — that is itself a signal.

rule.statement: Every send logs: engagement_id, scenario_used (one of the 7 indexed scenarios), phrasing_id, client_response (within 7 days: accepted-change-order | declined | silent | counter-proposed), and outcome ($ recovered or absorbed). Unlogged sends are not part of the practice.
rule.rationale: The pack improves only if its outcomes are tracked. The 7-day response classification surfaces which scenarios consistently convert and which always go silent; the $ outcome closes the loop between phrasing choice and engagement margin.

rule.statement: If the Applies-If preconditions fail OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or to no methodology at all).
rule.rationale: Mis-application of a methodology produces worse artefacts than no methodology at all because downstream consumers trust the schema and act on bad data. The skip-record makes the non-application traceable so future audits can detect drift.

example.description: Indexed phrasing for the "tiny tweak" scenario, with rule-conformance.
example.code:
### Scenario: "Tiny tweak" — client asks for an apparently small change that is actually out of scope
**Use as-is, replace [bracketed] parts:**
> Hi [name], > > Good catch on [the field change] — that would definitely make [the thing] cleaner. > (r1 acknowledge) > > Looking at our SOW for [engagement-name], this lands outside the [section X] we > scoped, so I'd want to handle it through a small change order so it doesn't > squeeze the items we already committed to this sprint. > (r2 reframe-as-change-order) > > Two options: > • Quick add to scope: I'll send a 1-line change order at our [rate]; usually > ~[N] hours of effort end-to-end. > • Defer to phase 2: I'll add it to the v2 backlog with your note attached. > (r2 concrete next step) > > If it helps, happy to jump on 15 minutes tomorrow to pick the path. > (escalation option per r4) > > — [founder]
phrasing_id: tiny-tweak-v1 last_used_with: acme-co (2026-04-12) last_outcome: accepted-change-order, $480 recovered
